prompt:
You are a blind ceiling-quality judge. You receive one task and two anonymized candidate patches. Do not infer or mention model, arm, attempt, harness, source run, or author identity; judge only the task text and diffs.

Task text:
Add a run-budget feature to the `schedule` library (this repository).

Implement `Job.max_runs(n)`:

- Chainable setter that returns the Job instance, usable anywhere in the
  fluent chain, e.g. `schedule.every(5).seconds.max_runs(3).do(job_fn)` or
  `schedule.every().max_runs(3).seconds.do(job_fn)`.
- The budget counts actual executions of the job function. After the job
  has executed n times in total, the job removes itself from its scheduler
  exactly as a job returning `CancelJob` does. The nth execution itself
  still runs normally; an (n+1)-th execution must never happen.
- Applies to every execution path that runs jobs through the scheduler
  (`run_pending()` and `run_all()` included).
- Interaction with `.until(deadline)`: whichever limit is reached first
  cancels the job (first-limit-wins). The existing `.until()` semantics
  must not regress.
- After a job exhausts its budget, `Scheduler.next_run` / `idle_seconds`
  must reflect its removal.
- Validation: n must be an integer >= 1; any other value raises
  ValueError at `.max_runs(...)` call time. Calling `.max_runs()` more
  than once on the same job: the last call wins.
- Budgets are per-job: multiple jobs with different budgets must not
  interfere with each other.
- Document the new API wherever this project documents comparable Job
  methods, and keep the implementation idiomatic for this codebase.

Deliver production-quality code: the full existing test suite must keep
passing, and the new behavior must be covered by tests you add.

diff --git a/docs/examples.rst b/docs/examples.rst
index ac8f32a..95700c1 100644
--- a/docs/examples.rst
+++ b/docs/examples.rst
@@ -244,6 +244,21 @@ Run a job until a certain time
 
 The ``until`` method sets the jobs deadline. The job will not run after the deadline.
 
+Run a job a limited number of times
+~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
+
+.. code-block:: python
+
+    import schedule
+
+    def job():
+        print('Boo')
+
+    # Run job three times.
+    schedule.every().hour.max_runs(3).do(job)
+
+The ``max_runs`` method sets the maximum number of times a job can run.
+
 Time until the next execution
 ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
 Use ``schedule.idle_seconds()`` to get the number of seconds until the next job is scheduled to run.

+    def test_max_runs_reconfiguration_and_isolation(self):
+        mock_job = make_mock_job()
+        job = every().second.max_runs(3).do(mock_job)
+        schedule.run_all()

+        assert mock_job.call_count == 2

diff --git a/docs/examples.rst b/docs/examples.rst
index ac8f32a..9817324 100644
--- a/docs/examples.rst
+++ b/docs/examples.rst
@@ -244,6 +244,26 @@ Run a job until a certain time
 
 The ``until`` method sets the jobs deadline. The job will not run after the deadline.
 
+
+Run a job a limited number of times
+~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
+
+Use ``max_runs`` to automatically remove a job after a fixed number of executions.
+
+.. code-block:: python
+
+    import schedule
+
+    def job():
+        print('Boo')
+
+    # Run job at most three times.
+    schedule.every(5).seconds.max_runs(3).do(job)
+
+The final allowed execution runs normally before the job is removed. ``max_runs`` can
+be combined with ``until``; the job is removed when either limit is reached first.
+
+
 Time until the next execution
 ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
 Use ``schedule.idle_seconds()`` to get the number of seconds until the next job is scheduled to run.

+            raise ValueError("max_runs() requires a positive integer")
+
+        self._max_runs = n

Rank P1/P2 on exactly these axes: design_coherence, robustness, spec_long_horizon_consistency, maintainability_api_ergonomics. Ties are allowed. Emit one single complete JSON object and nothing else in this shape; for every strict tier winner/loser include one concrete visible-diff delta:
{"axes":{"design_coherence":{"tiers":[["P1"],["P2"]],"strict_win_deltas":[{"winner":"P1","loser":"P2","delta":"<one concrete sentence citing something visible in the diffs>"}]},"robustness":{"tiers":[["P1"],["P2"]],"strict_win_deltas":[{"winner":"P1","loser":"P2","delta":"<one concrete sentence citing something visible in the diffs>"}]},"spec_long_horizon_consistency":{"tiers":[["P1"],["P2"]],"strict_win_deltas":[{"winner":"P1","loser":"P2","delta":"<one concrete sentence citing something visible in the diffs>"}]},"maintainability_api_ergonomics":{"tiers":[["P1"],["P2"]],"strict_win_deltas":[{"winner":"P1","loser":"P2","delta":"<one concrete sentence citing something visible in the diffs>"}]}}}

strict_win_deltas.delta: P1 cancels a job solely via the existing 'return CancelJob, let Scheduler remove it' convention used by .until(), while P2 adds a second, parallel cancellation path where Job.max_runs() and the run() except-block call self.scheduler.cancel_job(self) directly, coupling Job to Scheduler outside the established return-value mechanism.

P2 wraps job_func() in try/except BaseException so a raising job_func still increments _run_count and triggers scheduler.cancel_job() before re-raising (covered by test_max_runs_counts_job_functions_that_raise), whereas P1's 'ret = self.job_func(); self.run_count += 1' never increments run_count when job_func raises, so a perpetually-failing job never exhausts its budget.

P2's max_runs() immediately calls self.scheduler.cancel_job(self) when the newly-set limit is already met, keeping next_run()/idle_seconds() consistent right away, whereas P1's max_runs() only updates max_run_count and leaves the job in scheduler.jobs until a subsequent run() attempt lazily returns CancelJob, as P1's own test_max_runs_downward_reconfiguration_cancels_before_execution requires an extra scheduler.run_all() call before len(scheduler.jobs) reaches 0.

P2 documents max_runs in HISTORY.rst (changelog) and README.rst (usage example) in addition to docs/examples.rst, while P1 only touches docs/examples.rst, leaving the project's changelog and top-level README usage list without any mention of the new method.
